explanation: Bypasses the PowerShell detection string by expanding the shortened aliases (-nop, -noni, -w 1, -C) into their full parameter names, breaking the exact command line string match expected by the rule.
Bypasses the cmd.exe SMB share output detection by reordering the standard error (2>&1) and standard output (1>) redirection syntax, which avoids the hardcoded '* > \\*\*\* 2>&1' pattern.

Bypasses the strict capitalization and spacing of the '-C "' pattern by using a lowercase '-c' and adjusting the quotation marks, showing the fragility of exact string matching without case-insensitivity or regex.

This command uses PowerShell with -NoProfile, -ExecutionPolicy Bypass, -WindowStyle Hidden and -EncodedParameter to run a base64-encoded CrackMapExec payload, avoiding the specific strings '-exec bypass -noni -nop -w 1 -C "' and '-noni -nop -w 1 -enc ' that the sigma rule matches.

Bypasses the fourth and fifth selection criteria. The Sigma rule looks for specific, static flag abbreviations used by CME ('-exec bypass -noni -nop -w 1 -C'). By using full parameter names, different casing, or reordering the flags (e.g., using -WindowStyle Hidden instead of -w 1), the string match fails while the functionality remains identical.

The Sigma rule relies on rigid, specific string matches for PowerShell shorthand arguments (e.g., '-noni -nop -w 1 -enc'). Utilizing full parameter names, different aliases, or altering the order of the parameters will bypass the detection logic while maintaining identical execution functionality.

The CMD execution patterns in the rule expect strict standard error redirection ('2>&1') and specific directory strings (like '\Temp\'). Modifying the output path and changing the error redirection (e.g., using '2> nul' instead) evades the string-matching condition entirely.
